title: Share of land defined as natural habitat
subtitle: Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.
caption:
Share of land defined as natural habitat

Natural ecosystems include forest, grassland, savanna, and shrublands, and other classes such as peatlands and marshes. It is total land minus land that is used by humans for agriculture – either croplands or pasture – or for infrastructure and urban environments.

The chart tracks the percentage of land defined as natural habitat from 1780 to 2010 for the World, Germany, France, and the United Kingdom. The World series begins at approximately 75% in 1780 and follows a consistent downward trajectory, reaching roughly 50% by 2010. In contrast, the national series for Germany, France, and the United Kingdom start between 45% and 65% in 1780 and generally decline until the mid-20th century. The United Kingdom line drops from roughly 64% to a low point near 28% around 1940, before rising sharply to finish near 48% in 2010. Germany and France follow similar patterns, bottoming out between 40% and 45% around 1950 before climbing steeply to end the period between 62% and 66%. All three European nations show a distinct V-shape recovery in the latter half of the timeline, whereas the global trend remains a continuous decline throughout the entire 230-year period. By 2010, the values for Germany and France exceed their 1780 starting points, while the United Kingdom remains slightly below its initial level, and the global average has decreased significantly from its starting position.
